paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
tei_path: /content/drive/MyDrive/content_analysis_epistemology/papers/tei/raw/psychology/Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV.tei.xml

metadata.title: A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV

abstract: With the growing number of older people living with HIV, "What is the most effective geriatric care and the research trend of existing literature?" is a compelling question after 30 years since the first paper related to aging and HIV/AIDS published. Our study aims to apply quantitative and qualitative analysis to explore the knowledge gaps and describes the research interest of gerontology research in the field of HIV. A bibliometric analysis was conducted based on the databased of the Web of Science from 1991 to 2019. The major domains of research areas were visualized by using VOSviewer software. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) was applied to classify the dataset into topics. There was a rising number of publications about this topic over time. Our findings indicated that antiretroviral treatment and evaluating quality of life and harm reduction were the major domains regarding care for OPLWH. In addition, the finding highlights the role of social competence in treatment outcomes. Further research needs to tailor multidisciplinary programs and flexible interventions to reduce the burden and the mortality rate of HIV/AIDS.

sections.paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
paragraphs: Since the introduction antiretroviral therapy (ART), the life expectancy and age of people living with HIV has continued to rise (Tran et al., 2020). More than 50% of people living with HIV in San Francisco, for example, are aged 50 or older (O'Keefe et al., 2013), and this number in the total population of the United States will be 73% by 2030 ("Senate Aging Committee Holds Hearing to Examine the Effects of HIV/AIDS on Older Americans," 2013). Globally, this prevalence is expected to increase, especially in low-middle income countries, where more than 80% of older people living with HIV (OPLWH) in 2016 live (Autenrieth et al., 2018). This trend is responsible for the high level of vulnerability in the HIV population. For instance, it increases the burden of age-related noncommunicable diseases, which is found to be higher than that of general populations, and the risk of adverse effects of polypharmacy and other complications of HIV treatment (Smit et al., 2015). Additionally, in the interview of 307 OPLWH, more than 30% reported being socially isolated, 82% complained about moderate to severe levels of loneliness, and 58% had experienced HIV self-stigma (Blowes, 2018). In the finding in resource limited settings in 2002, financial burden, stigma as well as violence have been reported in OPLWH (WHO, 2002).

It can be seen that the process of aging has a significant impact on the outcome of HIV/AIDS treatment, including delay diagnosis, immune senescence and the response to ART (Bamford et al., 2010;Gardner, 1980). Clearly, OPLWH are at higher risk of neurocognitive disorders, non-AIDS-defining diseases, HIVrelated disability and mortality as a result of prolong HIV infection, HIV treatment toxicity, and long-term use of ART (Egger et al., 2002;Marra et al., 2009;Phillips et al., 1991;Valcour et al., 2004). The prevalence of chronic diseases (i.e., Liver diseases, kidney diseases, osteoporosis, diabetes, and malignancy) are also higher in OPLWH compared to the general population (Clifford & Ances, 2013;Guerri-Fernandez et al., 2013;Janssen et al., 1992;Palella et al., 2006). Previous research found that there was a close relationship between the number of comorbidities and the risk of stress and depression among OPLWH (Balderson et al., 2013). OPLWH have more problems with usual activities, self-care and mobility compared to their HIV-negative peers (Miners et al., 2014). Therefore, this translates to the greater need to understand the pathophysiology of aging and address the guideline of geriatric (health care for older adults) to older people infected with HIV. Several priority tasks in light of management comorbidities for OPLWH have been updated including routine screening, comprehensive history, especially among woman age ≥ 50y (Aberg et al., 2014;Hopkins et al., 2020;Wing, 2016).

With the complex patterns of multimorbidity and frailty, geriatric or HIV management for OPLWH requires a wide range of disciplines such as sociology, gerontology, oncology, and cardiology (Justice & Braithwaite, 2012). A number of studies have discussed and updated successful aging models that are needed to provide holistic care for OPLWH. However, HIV is concerned as an expensive disease with the high cost of ART (rising from €9.08 million up to €9.10 million in 2015) (Taramasso et al., 2018). As a result, OPLWH might find it difficult to access geriatric services if they did not have health insurance or other financial supports (Crystal et al., 2003). In LMIC countries, for example Africa, there is also a lack of research attention towards disease burden and health care services for age-related diseases, despite of the fact that the number of OPLWH in this continent is increasing rapidly (Autenrieth et al., 2018). Of note, supporting and enhancing the NCDs service including prevention, disgnosis and treatment for OPLWH could reduce the burden of aging issues, since NCDs require long-term care. Therefore, it is important to understand, integrate contextualized factors, and tailor flexible HIV interventions for OPLWH.

There are a numerous number of reviews evaluating the different aspects of healthcare intervention (including clinical, psychological, behavioral and socio-medical aspects) in advancing the outcomes among people aging with HIV/AIDS (Brothers & Rockwood, 2018;Rueda et al., 2014;Sankar et al., 2011). However, these researches mostly analyze the healthcare models in certain research areas rather than providing a comprehensive picture of care using multidisciplinary approaches for OPLWH. This limitation could lead to underestimating the global contextual factors as well as context of healthcare services for OPLWH. Therefore, the current study uses an advanced technique that combines global mapping approaches and analysis content to examine the construction and presents the research profiles of older adults living with HIV research in the context of a rapid change of HIV epidemiology (Reuters, 2008). Our study aims to describe the trend and pattern regarding quantitative and qualitative of existing publications via citation index and the content of research in efforts to decrease the burden of OPLWH from 1991 to 2019 and explores the current knowledge gap as well as suggests further interventions and research in the field of HIV/AIDS.

More than 50% of people living with HIV in San Francisco, for example, are aged 50 or older (O'Keefe et al., 2013), and this number in the total population of the United States will be 73% by 2030 ("Senate Aging Committee Holds Hearing to Examine the Effects of HIV/AIDS on Older Americans," 2013). For instance, it increases the burden of age-related noncommunicable diseases, which is found to be higher than that of general populations, and the risk of adverse effects of polypharmacy and other complications of HIV treatment (Smit et al., 2015). sections.paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
paragraphs: This study conducted a cross-sectional searching on the database of Web of Science (WoS). As Web of Science Core Collection is the largest online library that included high impact scientific papers from 1900 that users can often retrieve easily (Clarivate, 2020;Martín-Martín et al., 2018). This database was purposefully selected for a content analysis and bibliometric to cover the topics of OPLWH. The search term was combination of keywords related to HIV and the elderly (Supplementary 1).

sections.paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
paragraphs: Step 1: An advanced literature search was conducted to find the relevant titles, abstracts, and all keywords in WoS with HIV/AIDS terms. Downloaded packages include the general information regarding the publication index (i.e., co-author' names paper's titles, the scientific journal title, author's affiliation, total number of citations, the frequency of downloaded times), keywords and abstract. . Step 2: We extracted papers that included the group terms of elderly which were review by specialists. The data, then, was converted to the format of Microsoft Excel software for cross-checking by two researchers. Publications that only mentioned the term "HIV/AIDS" as an example in the background or recommendation was removed. If there was any disagreements, they could be resolved by discussing with a senior researcher.

sections.paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
paragraphs: STATA version 15.0 (STATA Corp., Texas, United States) was used to conduct further analyze. With descriptive analysis of bibliometric, the general information including the following indicators: the speed of publication (the frequency of publication by years); the research trend (number of citation, mean cite rate per year) and scholar's usage (total downloaded times) in the last 6 months/5 years. Additionally, the main research clusters of OPLWH were generated based on the co-occurrence of most keywords and plus keywords (Annex 1). The VOSviewer (version 1.6.8, Center for Science and Technology, Leiden University, the Netherlands) software was employed to visualize the co-occurrence of most frequent terms and the country's collaboration networks.

As for quantitative analysis, the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model was used to identify the hierarchical clustering of 10 major research disciplines of the unstructured texts, in other words, it allows to classify group of similar meaning words into specific topics. In previous literature, LDA had been defined as a state-of-the-art or the most common method for topic modeling (Asmussen & Møller, 2019). All pooled publications are considered as K latent topics which consisted of a cluster of co-appeared words (Asmussen & Møller, 2019;DiMaggio et al., 2013). The LDA generative process is illustrated in Figure 1 (Kang et al., 2019):.

The α and η (as hyperparameters) and the number of topics (K) have to be defined prior to the LDA analysis. Value of α and η could decide the number of specific topics. Regarding K, there are two approaches to define the number of topics. The first method is calculating the log-likelihoods of different k values and compared them. The k value with the highest log-likelihood result is selected. This approach has been applied in some previous bibliometric studie (Griffiths & Steyvers, 2004;Kang et al., 2019;Syed & Weber, 2018).

In our study, for example, the words "senescence" and "antiretroviral" were assumed the paper on the topic of pharmacotherapy for people aging with HIV since it co-appeared frequently, while the topic related to quality of life for OPLWH was identified with the words "sexual", "behaviour" and "well-being". In addition, the number of trending topics were defined by using topic modeling. The extracted data was continuously allocated into 10 topics ranking in the table list based on the number of papers in the past 5 years from 2015 up to 2019 (Supplementary 2). Two experts in the field of gerontology and HIV were invited to review and label each topic in order to provide the most semantically meaningful interpretation.

sections.paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
paragraphs: Since the first four articles published in 1991, the annual volume of publication has increased over time and reached 114 papers in 2019 (Figure 2). The period of 5 years from 2010 to 2014 observed a trebling in the number of total citations and reached 1,699 citations, before going down again in the next years. Papers published in 2013 not only had the highest number of downloads in the last 5 years, about 729 times, but also had the highest mean use rate in the last 5 years. The mean citation rate per year of articles published in 2006 and 2007 were 4.29 and 4.10 higher than the number of the other years.

Figure 3 presents the level of international partnerships of 74 countries and regions, which have been categorized into 5 major clusters. The United States and South Africa have high prevalence of HIV/AIDS, and these countries are the major contributor to the total number of international publications related to HIV/ AIDS. It seem like the co-authors tend to have close relationships with each other according to the geographical location. For example, the red cluster indicates the collaboration between in African countries, the green cluster refers to European countries, and the yellow cluster presents countries in Asia.

By classifying co-occurrence terms into colors and size, the main research areas regarding aging and HIV manifest itself as 5 major clusters (Figure 4). There are 257 most popular keywords having co-occurrence rates of at least 6. The red cluster refers to pharmacological research related to antiretroviral therapy, including the long-term effects, immune senescence, and the adverse interaction consequences of multiple drugs of HIV patients. The yellow cluster presents the characteristics of the aging process in association with neuropsychological impairment and dementia. The purple cluster visualizes the main component of the quality of life for older adults living with HIV, while the green cluster displays to topics about mental health and the impact of social support on emotional wellbeing. Lastly, the blue cluster indicates sexual behaviors, such as using condoms among targeted population, for instance, older gay men.

Table 1 presents the papers that had the highest cited from 1991. The top 10 articles are concentrated on the aging process on the immune response as well as the mental health in OPLWH. Research of Shiels et al. in 2011 described the shift in the distribution of cancer among HIV-Infected individuals whose older age could coincide with increased non-AIDS-defining cancer. By describing the aging process at the micro-level, Fagnoni provided an insight into the impact of CD8(+) T cells on impairing the function of the immune system. The third paper, which was published in the Journal of Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndromes, introduced the fundamental knowledge about the relationship between age and HIV.

Based on LDA topic modeling, ten major topic headings are categorized in Table 2. Risk awareness and social care for elderly, sexual behaviors, harm reduction, and geriatric care were the top three topics having the largest number of publications. Accounting for 17.1% of total articles, there were a total 141 papers in Topic 1. The second position and third make up 14% and 12.4%, respectively.

Figure 5 refers to the dendrogram of aging research aiming to improve the well-being of OPLWH. While the horizontal axis presents the dissimilarity within clusters, the vertical axis reflects disciplines of pooled papers. There are 3 groups of research roots. The first group mentions immunology, infectious disease, and virology studies, which are basic topics for HIV treatment. The other aspects are about healthcare for the elderly (i.e., Geriatric, gerontology) in association with nursing care and multidisciplinary sciences. The last group covers not only clinical evidence for HIV treatment but also the research in public health, which includes the relationship between environmental, human, and policy.
sections.text:
Since the first four articles published in 1991, the annual volume of publication has increased over time and reached 114 papers in 2019 (Figure 2). The period of 5 years from 2010 to 2014 observed a trebling in the number of total citations and reached 1,699 citations, before going down again in the next years. Papers published in 2013 not only had the highest number of downloads in the last 5 years, about 729 times, but also had the highest mean use rate in the last 5 years. The mean citation rate per year of articles published in 2006 and 2007 were 4.29 and 4.10 higher than the number of the other years.

Figure 3 presents the level of international partnerships of 74 countries and regions, which have been categorized into 5 major clusters. The United States and South Africa have high prevalence of HIV/AIDS, and these countries are the major contributor to the total number of international publications related to HIV/ AIDS. It seem like the co-authors tend to have close relationships with each other according to the geographical location. For example, the red cluster indicates the collaboration between in African countries, the green cluster refers to European countries, and the yellow cluster presents countries in Asia.

By classifying co-occurrence terms into colors and size, the main research areas regarding aging and HIV manifest itself as 5 major clusters (Figure 4). There are 257 most popular keywords having co-occurrence rates of at least 6. The red cluster refers to pharmacological research related to antiretroviral therapy, including the long-term effects, immune senescence, and the adverse interaction consequences of multiple drugs of HIV patients. The yellow cluster presents the characteristics of the aging process in association with neuropsychological impairment and dementia. The purple cluster visualizes the main component of the quality of life for older adults living with HIV, while the green cluster displays to topics about mental health and the impact of social support on emotional wellbeing. Lastly, the blue cluster indicates sexual behaviors, such as using condoms among targeted population, for instance, older gay men.

The first group mentions immunology, infectious disease, and virology studies, which are basic topics for HIV treatment. The other aspects are about healthcare for the elderly (i.e., Geriatric, gerontology) in association with nursing care and multidisciplinary sciences. The last group covers not only clinical evidence for HIV treatment but also the research in public health, which includes the relationship between environmental, human, and policy.

sections.paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
paragraphs: This study provides a comprehensive snapshot reflecting a wide range of interdisciplinary regarding gerontology and HIV research during the period from 1991 to 2019. Overall, there was a rising number of publications about this topic. Our finding indicates that antiretroviral treatment and evaluating quality of life and harm reduction were the major domains regarding care for OPLWH. In addition, there was a lack of research evaluating the effectiveness of local funding, contextualized factors, and integrated healthcare management with social networks.

This study points out the growing interest of aging and HIV through a quantities of publications in response to the rising number of people aged 50 or older who have achieved and maintained viral load with ARV in undetectable levels. At the 18th International AIDS Conference in 2010, over 20,000 scholars recognized this topic as the leading issues surrounding HIV and AIDS care (Catz et al., 2010). As a result, there was significant attention (i.e., More citation) coinciding with more usage in the last 5 years (from 2010 to 2014). In addition, 30 years after the first publication related to the influence of age on AIDS progression, the definition of "aging" is widely adopted as an indicator to evaluating the success of HIV treatment ART and quality of life are the most common keywords in the existing literature. It can be seen that there is a strong relationship between ART and the well-being of PLWH in various domains. Mothers and children, for example, are known two groups that received the major response with different ART goals (Mills et al., 2012). While the prevention of motherto-child transmission is the primary HIV action, developing protocols, guidline for peadiatric care or designed psychological support HIV disclosing is the most importance strategy to encourage the early ART treatment in children (WHO, 2019;Vreeman et al., 2013). To date, in comparison to these vulnerable, innocent and deserving of protection subjects, OPLWH seem to be neglected in the management of HIV/AIDS eventhough the number of PLWH acquiring their infection at age 50 and above rapidly increase ("Senate Aging Committee Holds Hearing to Examine the Effects of HIV/AIDS on Older Americans," 2013). Indeed, ART has a vital role in the "greying" of the HIV population, this term commonly appears in diverse research areas such as immunology, virology, biology, health policy, etc. Previous literature pointed out that advanced ART not only has become more effective and better tolerated (Gallant et al., 2006;Lange, 2003;Montaner et al., 2006;van Leth et al., 2004) but also requires multidisciplinary care and treatment to optimize adherence and thus, enhance the response of the immune system to such pharmaceutical treatment (Horberg et al., 2012;Perez & Moore, 2003). This development results in higher expectations of a better quality of life consisting of a variety of human needs of goals, standards, and expectations (Dennison, 2002). Indeed, many researchers have demonstrated the relationship between the effectiveness of ART and the level of quality of life (Alec Miners et al., 2014). Additionally, due to the natural aging, caring for OPLWH now comes with the additional complexity of managing comorbidities, frailty, and cognitive impairment (Balderson et al., 2013;Brothers & Rockwood, 2018;Valcour et al., 2004). Improving ART adherence among older adults is demonstrated that it could remain effective and makes older adults enjoy a normal life and adopt successful aging (Catz et al., 2010). However, the availability of healthcare services, cultural norms, personal beliefs, and stigma serve as the challenges of the level of adherence in OPLWH. This finding suggests the necessary cultural context and social factors in the theoretical framework of intervention for maximizing the adherence and treatment outcomes among older people.

Our study confirms the expansion of HIV/AIDS interventions in terms of coping strategies and harm reduction among OPLWH in the past 5 years. While there has been increased life expectancy of individuals with HIV, the prevalence of mental distress in this population remains high. Self-blaming, social isolation, physical, verbal abuse, mistreating, and political discrimination, which is frequently reported in PLWH, could increase the prevalence of depression or anxiety, and impacts treatment adherence (Prasitsuebsai et al., 2018;Pulerwitz et al., 2015). Furthermore, two primary sources of stigma (including related to that of HIV/ AIDS and that of ageism) contribute to higher rates of substance use in OPLWH compared to their HIV-negative peers (Emlet, 2006). There have been numerous interventions supporting older adults with HIV, including treatments, coping strategies, and harm reduction (Deren et al., 2019;Edelman et al., 2014;Green et al., 2010;Moore et al., 2013). However, these studies did not evaluate the effectiveness of community activities and the aging social network within the support systems. In later life, seeing that adults aging with HIV are increasingly susceptible to shrinking social networks (i.e., Loss of friends, health status) (Shippy & Karpiak, 2005), it is necessary to address informal social networks and multiple health care managements beyond a focus on viral suppression.

This study integrates an extensive bibliometric of all searchable research. Some limitations, however, should be acknowledged. First, the publication in the period from 1981 to 1990 were not included in our dataset due to use of the Web of Science. Second, the finding might not cover all publications, such as grey literature. Third, the small number of papers from non-Western countries were excluded in the scientometric analysis due to the included criteria of English language. However, it can be seen that the majority of high-quality studies are published in English; therefore, language restriction might increase the quality of findings.

sections.paper_id: Hoang et al. - 2022 - A global analysis of literature on older adults and lifelong living with HIV
paragraphs: In this study, we aim to evaluate the publication trends and elucidate the main research construct in terms of older adults with HIV via scientometric analysis. Our finding suggests an important role of geriatric and gerontology in relation to HIV/AIDS. Further research needs to tailor a multi-disciplinary programs and flexible interventions to reduce the burden and the mortality rate of HIV/AIDS.
